title: NA-ULIP
word_text:
ULIP vs Mutual Funds: Which is Better
ULIPs and mutual funds are both popular long-term investment avenues but cater to different financial goals. Unit Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs) are a type of life insurance product which combines life insurance with investment in which a portion of the premiums paid for the policy is used to provide life cover and the remaining is investment in market-linked funds such as equity, debt or balanced funds based on your risk appetite and financial goals. Mutual funds, on the other hand pools the money from a group of investors and put it in several market linked assets such as equities, debt, or hybrid funds. They do not have any insurance coverage and are pure investment products for long-term gains.
To understand, ULIP vs MF, it is essential to understand, what is ULIP, its advantages, what is mutual funds, its advantages, difference between both in terms of returns, insurance cover, and risk involved to make a right choice between both depending on your insurance needs, risk appetite, liquidity and financial goals.
What is ULIP?
A ULIP is a financial product which combines life insurance and investment in a single plan. It provides dual benefit of protection and wealth creation in one to fulfil your long-term goals. The premiums of the plan are partially used towards life cover and partially invested in the funds that you choose. Investors can also switch between different fund options during the tenure of the policy, and adjust their investments with the changing market conditions. They come with a lock-in period (usually five years), encouraging disciplined long-term investing.
In the unfortunate event of the premature death of the insured during the policy term, ULIPs provide a death benefit to the beneficiaries. On maturity, the fund value is paid.
Advantages of ULIP
Some advantages of ULIPs are as follows –
Insurance coverage
Being a life insurance policy, ULIPs provide financial coverage against the risk of premature demise. if the life assured passes away during the policy tenure, a death benefit is paid which can help the family deal with the financial loss suffered. Moreover, if the life assured had unfulfilled financial goals or an existing debt, the death benefit can be used to fulfil the goal or pay off the debt. Thus, besides investments, ULIPs also provide financial security to the life assured that in his absence, the plan would help the family face financial loss.
Market-linked returns
With ULIPs, you can participate in the market and earn market-linked returns on your investments. If the markets are performing well, your investments can grow considerably and give you a good corpus for your financial goals. Moreover, if you choose a long-term tenure, the market-linked returns can grow your corpus considerably with the benefit of compounding.
Professional fund management
The fund options available under ULIPs are managed professionally by expert fund managers. They pick the securities that comprise the fund portfolio. Moreover, they buy and sell securities to ensure that the portfolio performs well and delivers good returns while minimising investment risks.
Flexibility
ULIPs offer a high degree of flexibility so that you can customise the plan per your needs and also have complete control over your investments. Some of the flexible features under ULIPs are as follows –
Choice of plan details –
You can choose the premium that you want to invest (subject to the minimum and maximum limits prescribed), the premium payment term and frequency, the sum assured (if a choice of sum assured multiple is provided), the funds to invest in, the policy tenure and the riders to add to the policy.
Switching –
ULIPs give you the freedom to change the investment funds if your investment strategy changes. This is called switching and the facility is allowed under all ULIPs. With switching, you can change the funds to limit your losses or increase your gains. For instance, if the equity market turns volatile, you can switch your equity funds to debt funds to protect your investments from volatility.
Partial withdrawals –
Partial withdrawals allow you to withdraw from the fund value during the policy tenure. ULIPs have a lock-in period of 5 years after which you can make partial withdrawals for your financial needs and get easy liquidity.
Premium redirection –
Premium redirection means changing the future premium allocation to another fund. You can opt for the redirection feature to invest your premium in other funds offered by the ULIP.
Top-up premiums –
Top-ups allow you to invest more in the ULIP if you want to boost your investments. Depending on the ULIP selected, you can pay top-up premiums and boost the fund value to create a higher corpus for your financial goals.
Tax Benefits
To add the cherry to the cake, ULIPs are highly tax-efficient. Here’s how –
a. The premium paid for the policy qualifies as a deduction under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act of 1961 under old regime subject to conditions stated therein. The maximum deduction allowed is Rs.1.5 lakhs. To claim the deduction, the maximum allowed premium is defined as follows –
i. 20% of the capital sum assured for policies issued on or before 31st March 20121
ii. 10% of the sum assured for policies issued on or after 1st April 20121
iii. 15% of the capital sum assured for policies issued on or after 1st April 2013 if the life assured suffers from a disease or disability defined under Sections 80U or 80DDB, respectively1.
b. The death benefit received is always tax-free2.
c. The maturity benefit received from ULIPs is also tax-free under Section 10(10D) if the premium is up to 10%, 15% or 20% of the sum assured depending on when the policy was issued3.
d. However, for policies issued on or after 1st February 2021, the maturity benefit will be tax-free under Section 10(10D) if the aggregate annual premium paid for all ULIPs is up to Rs.2.5 lakhs3.
e. Switching and partial withdrawals are also tax-free4 and 5.
What are Mutual funds?
Mutual funds are pooled investment avenues which collect investments from different investors and pool the investment into a corpus. The corpus is, then, allocated to different types of market-linked securities depending on the investment objective of the fund.
There are different types of mutual funds and you can choose a fund which suits your financial needs, investment strategy and risk appetite. You can invest in mutual funds through a lump sum or in instalments via SIPs (Systematic Investment Plans).
Advantages of Mutual funds
Some of the advantages of mutual funds are as follows –
Market-linked returns
Mutual funds are market-linked avenues which invest in a variety of securities like equity, debt, arbitrage, gold, indices, etc. With mutual funds you can get market-linked returns which can help you grow a good corpus for your goals.
Professional fund management
Mutual funds are managed by expert fund managers who manage the portfolio to enhance its return potential while reducing the investment risks. So, with mutual funds you can get the benefit of professional fund management.
Easy liquidity
Other than some mutual funds (Equity Linked Saving Schemes, Fixed Maturity Plans in debt funds close-ended funds, etc.)6, there’s no lock-in period. You can redeem your investment any time and convert it into liquid cash. This comes in handy for meeting financial emergencies.
Affordable investments
Mutual funds are pocket-friendly investment avenues wherein you can start a Systematic Investment Plan (SIP) with as little as Rs.100 per month7. Thus, mutual funds are suitable for small investors too who have a limited saving at their disposal.
Tax benefits
If you choose the ELSS scheme of mutual funds, you can claim a tax deduction under Section 80C of Income Tax Act 1961, under old regime, subject to provisions stated therein, up to Rs.1.5 lakhs on the invested amount. This helps you reduce your tax liability. Moreover, long-term gains from equity are tax-free up to Rs.1 lakh a year.
Differences between ULIP & Mutual funds
ULIP & Mutual funds differs from each other on the basis of use and various other factors, stated as follows:
Insurance coverage
ULIP plans have in-built life insurance cover in the plan. In which, a death benefit is payable to the beneficiaries of the policy in the event of unfortunate death of life insured during the term of the plan. However, such coverage is not available in mutual funds as they are purely investment vehicles.
Lock-in period
ULIPs comes with a prefixed lock-in period of 5 years during which the policy can be surrendered but surrender charges apply. Moreover, the surrender value, if any, is also payable upon the expiry of the lock-in period of 5 years. Partial withdrawals are also disallowed during the lock-in period. Whereas mutual funds do not have any lock-in period (except ELSS, which have a lock-in period of 3 years) and can be sold at your discretion and liquified.
Loyalty additions or fund boosters
Some ULIPs offer additional returns as loyalty additions, wealth boosters or fund boosters, which are usually declared at a fixed rate. These additional returns help enhance the value of fund. The returns from ULIPS depend on the market performance of the underlying fund. Mutual funds, on the other hand, usually don’t have any loyalty additions or fund boosters, the returns depend solely on market performance.
Investment tenure
ULIP plans come with a policy tenure. This tenure can start from 5 years and run lifelong (99 or 100 years under whole life ULIPs). Besides the lock-in period under some, mutual funds have no such tenure. You can stay invested for as long as you want.
Switching
ULIPs allow switching which helps you change between investment funds. This switching does not affect the policy and is also tax-free. There’s no concept of switching in mutual funds. If you want to change funds, you will have to sell or redeem the existing fund and then invest in another fund. This might attract applicable tax implications on the profits made from redeeming a fund.
Partial withdrawals
ULIPs allow partial withdrawals which help you draw from the fund value for your financial needs. Such withdrawals are tax-free. There’s no facility of partial withdrawals under mutual funds. If you withdraw from a fund, it will be treated at redemption which might be subject to tax implication too.
Tax implication
ULIPs offer tax benefits under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, subject to the terms and conditions stated therein. The death benefit is tax-free and the maturity benefit also enjoys tax exemption if the ULIP was purchased before February 1, 2021. However, ULIPs purchased on or after February 1, 2021, will have a different set of ULIP maturity taxability rules, as specified under the Income Tax Act, 1961.
In case of mutual funds, ELSS offers tax benefits under Section 80C. The returns earned from mutual funds, including any dividend, might attract taxation depending on your investment tenure and the type of fund that you have invested in.
Let’s take a look at the comparison between the two in a table as below:
What Factors to Consider Before Investing in ULIPs or Mutual Funds?
Choosing the right investment option between ULIP vs MF requires careful analysis of your risk appetite, financial goals and time horizon. Before deciding to invest in ULIPs or Mutual Funds, consider the following key factors to ensure your choice aligns with your long-term objectives.
Tax Advantages
When comparing ULIP vs MF, tax advantages are a major differentiator. ULIP plans provides tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per year on premiums paid under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, decreasing the taxable income. Additionally, maturity and death benefits are also tax-exempt under Section 10(10D), subject to specific conditions.
In contrast, only ELSS mutual funds qualify tax benefits under Section 80C of up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum.
Investment Portfolio Flexibility
ULIPs offer a choice of equity, debt, hybrid, and other types of funds based on financial objectives and risk appetite. Plus, switching helps you change between investment funds.
Mutual funds, on the other hand, offer different types of funds but switching is not available.
Risk Assessment
In terms of risk, market risks are similar across ULIPs and mutual funds and depend on the type of fund chosen. Both invest in market-linked securities, and the risk profile depends on the fund that you choose.
Common Misconceptions About ULIP and Mutual Fund
Here are some of the common misconceptions people have about ULIP and mutual fund:
Many people believe that since ULIP plans are market-linked, the life cover fluctuates with market performance. However, the life cover in ULIP plans remains fixed, and in the event of death, the specified death benefit is paid.
People think mutual funds are only for experts. However, in reality, mutual funds are designed for all kinds of investors.
It’s commonly assumed that ULIPs and mutual funds are risky. While returns are market-linked, you can choose debt funds to minimize the inherent risks.
People often believe mutual funds lock the money, but except for ELSS (which has a fixed lock-in period of 3 years) and other close-ended schemes, most mutual funds allow easy entry and exit.
Key Takeaways
Mutual Funds are pure investment products which offers high liquidity, flexibility, and potentially higher returns.
ULIP plans provide dual benefits of insurance and investment, tax efficiency, and easy fund-switching.
Mutual Funds (except tax-saving funds) does not have any lock-in period whereas ULIPs have a mandatory lock-in period, usually five years.
ULIPs provide tax benefits under Sections 80C and 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961 whereas mutual funds (ELSS only) offer limited tax deductions under 80C.
ULIPs are suitable for long-term, goal-based investors having low liquidity needs and mutual funds are suitable for those seeking flexibility and short- to medium-term options for creating a corpus for their goals.
Conclusion
ULIP and mutual funds have different uses and benefits and must match the unique individual needs. The choice between ULIP vs MF depends on your risk tolerance and financial goals. ULIPs are ideal for investors who are looking for a combination of life cover, long-term investment and tax advantages under one plan. On the other hand, mutual funds are suitable for investors who are looking for greater liquidity, flexibility, and a variable investment period. Understanding the key differences in ULIP vs MF helps you to align your investment with your financial strategy and make an informed investment decision.
FAQs
What is the lock-in period under ELSS schemes?
ELSS mutual funds have a lock-in period of 3 years.
How are mutual funds taxed?
Mutual funds are taxed based on the type of fund selected and the investment tenure. If you choose a fund which invests at least 65% of its portfolio in equity, the fund will be called an equity fund. Gains from equity funds would be called short-term capital gains if you redeem the fund within 12 months. Such gains would be taxed at 15%. However, if you redeem after 12 months, the gains earned would be called long-term capital gains. Such gains would be tax-free up to Rs.1 lakh a year and excess gains would be taxed at a flat rate of 10%8. Capital gains earned from debt funds will be taxed at your income tax slab rates irrespective of the holding tenure8.
Is the Section 80C tax benefit for ULIPs available under the new tax regime?
The new tax regime does not allow deduction under Section 80C for premiums paid for ULIPs. If you want to claim 80C deduction, you should file your taxes under the old tax regime.
Can I invest in both ULIP and Mutual Funds at the same time?
Yes, you can invest in both ULIP and Mutual Funds at the same time. Many investors use ULIP Plans for insurance and disciplined long-term savings, and invest in mutual funds for flexible wealth creation.
Which is more suitable for long-term wealth creation — ULIP or Mutual Fund?
Both ULIPs and mutual funds can offer long-term wealth creation. However, if you want the added benefits of life insurance coverage and the flexibility of tax-free switching between funds, ULIPs can be a better choice.
How does the lock-in period impact my investment choice?
ULIPs have a lock-in of 5 years during which you cannot withdraw money during this time. This encourages disciplined savings but decreases liquidity. On the other hand, mutual fund investors can redeem their units at any time without any lock-in, except in the case of ELSS (which has a 3-year lock-in) or closed-ended mutual funds. So, choose an avenue that matches your investment horizon.
Which option is better for tax savings — ULIP or Mutual Fund ELSS?
Premiums in ULIP plans are eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Additionally, death or maturity proceeds are also tax-free subject to conditions stated therein. ELSS mutual funds are also eligible for tax exemptions under Section 80C up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum. However, returns from ELSS mutual funds are taxed above Rs. 1.25 lakhs as LTCG (Long term capital gains) at 12.5% on redemption. Hence, if you want tax-free maturity proceeds, subject to specific conditions, ULIP may suit you.
Is it possible to convert a ULIP into a regular mutual fund investment?
No, you cannot directly convert a ULIP into a regular mutual fund investment, since a ULIP is an insurance-cum-investment product regulated by IRDAI, while mutual funds are regulated by SEBI. So, choose a product depending on your needs and financial goals.
Disclaimer:
IN THIS POLICY, THE INVESTMENT RISK IN INVESTMENT PORTFOLIO IS BORNE BY THE POLICYHOLDER.
The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. The various funds offered under this contract are the names of the funds and do not in any way indicate the quality of these plans, their future prospects and returns.
The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions. Let’s take a look at the comparison between the two in a table as below:
Comparison point ULIP Mutual funds Investment purpose Insurance and investment Investment
Mode of payment
Premiums which can be paid once (single premium), for a limited tenure (limited premium) or throughout the policy tenure (regular premiums). Moreover, you can get different premium payment frequencies like annual, half-yearly, quarterly and monthly
Lump sum or SIP at periodic frequencies
Charges
A range of charges are applicable to the investor, like premium allocation charge, policy administration charge, fund management charge, mortality charge, etc.
Investor may have to bear an entry load or exit load. Other charges, if any, are deducted in the form of Total Expense Ratio from the invested amount.
Regulatory body
Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)
Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
Minimum investment
Depends on the type of policy selected
Depends on the type of mutual fund selected
What Factors to Consider Before Investing in ULIPs or Mutual Funds?
Choosing the right option between ULIP vs MF requires careful analysis of your risk appetite, financial goals and time horizon. Before deciding to invest in ULIPs or Mutual Funds, consider the following key factors to ensure your choice aligns with your long-term objectives.
● Tax Advantages
When deciding between ULIP vs MF, tax advantages are a major differentiator. ULIP plans provides tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per year on premiums paid under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, decreasing the taxable income. Additionally, maturity and death benefits are also tax-exempt under Section 10(10D), subject to specific conditions.
In contrast, only ELSS mutual funds qualify tax benefits under Section 80C of up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum. 1
● Investment Portfolio Flexibility
ULIPs offer a choice of market linked funds such as equity, debt, hybrid funds based on financial objectives and risk appetite. Plus, switching helps you change between different market linked funds.
● Risk Assessment
When it comes to investment aspect of ULIPs, market-based risk is assoicated along with growth potential. In a nutshell, market volatility will equally affect mutual funds and ULIPs. Both invest in market-linked securities, and the risk profile depends on the fund that you choose.
Common Misconceptions About ULIP and Mutual Fund
Here are some of the common misconceptions people have about ULIP and mutual fund:
● Many people believe that since ULIP plans are market-linked, life cover fluctuates with market performance. However, the life cover in ULIP plans remains fixed, and in the event of death, the specified death benefit is paid. ● People think mutual funds are only for experts. However, in reality, mutual funds are designed for all kinds of investors. ● It’s commonly assumed that ULIPs and mutual funds are risky. While returns are market-linked, you can choose debt funds to minimize the inherent risks. ● People often believe mutual funds lock the money, but except for ELSS (which has a fixed lock-in period of 3 years) and other closed-ended schemes, most mutual funds allow flexibility in investment and withdrawal.
Key Takeaways
● Mutual Funds are pure investment products which offer high liquidity, flexibility, and potentially higher returns. ● ULIP plans provide dual benefits of life insurance and market-linked investment, tax efficiency, and easy fund-switching. ● Mutual Funds (except tax-saving funds) does not have any lock-in period whereas ULIPs have a mandatory lock-in period of five years.
● ULIPs provide tax benefits under Sections 80C and 10(10D) of the Income Tax Act, 1961 whereas mutual funds (ELSS only) offer limited tax deductions under 80C. Note: Tax benefits under Section 80C are available only under the old tax regime. ● ULIPs are suitable for long-term, goal-based investors having low liquidity needs, and mutual funds are suitable for those seeking flexibility and short- to medium-term options for creating a corpus for their goals.
Conclusion
ULIP and mutual funds have different uses and benefits. It is preferable to choose an option after evaluating your current financial situation and future goals. The choice between ULIP vs MF depends on your risk tolerance and financial goals. ULIPs are suitable for investors who are looking for a combination of life cover, long-term investment and tax advantages under one plan. On the other hand, mutual funds are considered by individuals who are looking for greater liquidity, flexibility, and a variable investment period. Understanding the key differences in ULIP vs MF helps you to align your investment with your financial strategy and make an informed investment decision.
FAQs
What is the lock-in period under ELSS schemes?
ELSS mutual funds have a lock-in period of 3 years.
How are mutual funds taxed?
Mutual funds are taxed based on the type of fund selected and the investment tenure. If you choose a fund which invests at least 65% of its portfolio in equity, the fund will be called an equity fund. Gains from equity funds would be called short-term capital gains if you redeem the fund within 12 months. Such gains would be taxed at 15%. However, if you redeem after 12 months, the gains earned would be called long- term capital gains. Such gains would be tax-free up to Rs.1 lakh a year and excess gains would be taxed at a flat rate of 10%8. Capital gains earned from debt funds will be taxed at your income tax slab rates irrespective of the holding tenure8.
Is the Section 80C tax benefit for ULIPs available under the new tax regime?
The new tax regime does not allow deduction under Section 80C for premiums paid for ULIPs. If you want to claim 80C deduction, you should file your taxes under the old tax regime.
Can I invest in both ULIP and Mutual Funds at the same time?
Yes, you may invest in both ULIP and Mutual Funds at the same time. Many investors use ULIP Plans for life insurance and disciplined long-term savings, and invest in mutual funds for flexible wealth creation.
Which can be suitable for long-term wealth creation — ULIP or Mutual Fund?
Both ULIPs and mutual funds can offer long-term wealth creation, it depends upon needs of the buyer. However, if you want the added benefits of life insurance coverage and the flexibility of tax-free switching between funds, ULIPs can be taken into consideration.
How does the lock-in period impact my investment choice?
ULIPs have a lock-in of 5 years during which you cannot make partial withdrawals . This encourages disciplined savings but decreases liquidity. On the other hand, mutual fund investors can redeem their units at any time without any lock-in, except in the case of ELSS (which has a 3-year lock-in) or closed- ended mutual funds. So, choose an avenue that matches your investment horizon.
Which option is better for tax savings — ULIP or Mutual Fund ELSS?
Premiums in ULIP plans are eligible for tax deductions up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum under Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Additionally, death or maturity proceeds are also tax-free subject to conditions stated therein. ELSS mutual funds are also eligible for tax exemptions under Section 80C up to ₹1.5 lakh per annum. However, returns from ELSS mutual funds are taxed above ₹1.25 lakh as LTCG (Long term capital gains) at 12.5% on redemption. Hence, if you want tax-free maturity proceeds, subject to specific conditions, ULIP may suit you. 1
Is it possible to convert a ULIP into a regular mutual fund investment?
No, you cannot directly convert a ULIP into a regular mutual fund investment, since a ULIP is an insurance-cum-investment product regulated by IRDAI, while mutual funds are regulated by SEBI. So, choose a product depending on your needs and financial goals.

The Unit Linked Insurance Products do not offer any liquidity during the first five years of the contract. The policyholder will not be able to surrender or withdraw the monies invested in Unit Linked Insurance Products completely or partially till the end of the fifth year.
ULIPs are different from the traditional insurance products and are subject to the risk factors. The premium paid in ULIPs are subject to investment risks associated with capital markets and the NAVs of the units may go up or down based on the performance of fund and factors influencing the capital market and the insured is responsible for his/her decisions. Please know the associated risks and the applicable charges, from your Insurance agent or the Intermediary or policy document issued by the insurance company. changes.word_original: A ULIP is a financial product which combines life insurance and investment in a single plan.
changes.pdf_final: A ULIP is a life insurance plan which combines life insurance and market-linked investment in a single plan.

changes.word_original: It provides dual benefit of protection and wealth creation in one to fulfil your long-term goals.
changes.pdf_final: It provides dual benefits of life cover and the potential of wealth creation in one to fulfil your long- term goals.

changes.word_original: The premiums of the plan are partially used towards life cover and partially invested in the funds that you choose.
changes.pdf_final: The premiums of the plan are partially used towards life cover and partially invested in the market-linked funds that you choose.

changes.word_original: Investors can also switch between different fund options during the tenure of the policy, and adjust their investments with the changing market conditions.
changes.pdf_final: Policyholders can also switch between different fund options during the tenure of the policy, and adjust their investments with the changing market conditions.

changes.word_original: They come with a lock-in period (usually five years), encouraging disciplined long-term investing.
changes.pdf_final: They come up with a lock-in period of five years encouraging disciplined long-term investing.

changes.word_original: In the unfortunate event of the premature death of the insured during the policy term, ULIPs provide a death benefit to the beneficiaries.
changes.pdf_final: In the unfortunate event of the premature death of the life assured during the policy term, ULIPs provide a death benefit to the nominees .

changes.word_original: ULIPs have a lock-in period of 5 years after which you can make partial withdrawals for your financial needs and get easy liquidity.
changes.pdf_final: ULIPs have a lock- in period of 5 years after which you can make partial withdrawals for your financial needs and get easy liquidity.

changes.word_original: Premium redirection means changing the future premium allocation to another fund.
changes.pdf_final: Premium redirection means changing the future premium allocation to another fund.

changes.word_original: Insurance coverage
changes.pdf_final: Life Insurance coverage

changes.word_original: In which, a death benefit is payable to the beneficiaries of the policy in the event of unfortunate death of life insured during the term of the plan.
changes.pdf_final: In which, a death benefit is payable to the nominees of the policy in the event of unfortunate death of life assured during the term of the plan.

changes.word_original: These additional returns help enhance the value of fund.
changes.pdf_final: These additional returns help enhance the value of funds.
Comparison point ULIP Mutual funds Investment purpose Insurance and investment Investment
Mode of payment
Premiums which can be paid once (single premium), for a limited tenure (limited premium) or throughout the policy tenure (regular premiums).
Moreover, you can get different premium payment frequencies like annual, half-yearly, quarterly and monthly
Lump sum or SIP at periodic frequencies
Charges
A range of charges are applicable to the investor, like premium allocation charge, policy administration charge, fund management charge, mortality charge, etc.
Investor may have to bear an entry load or exit load.
Other charges, if any, are deducted in the form of Total Expense Ratio from the invested amount.
Regulatory body
Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI)
Securities and Exchange Board of India (SEBI)
Minimum investment
Depends on the type of policy selected
Depends on the type of mutual fund selected
